question_title: What percentage of Italy's natural gas storage capacity will be filled as of May 15, 2026, according to AGSI?
summary: Italy's gas storage fill percentage as of May 15, 2026 is forecast based on 15 days of actual AGSI injection data (April 20 - May 4). As of May 4, Italy was at 51.38% full with a very stable recent injection rate of ~0.36-0.38 pp/day (~765 GWh/day). Over the remaining 11 days to May 15, linear extrapolation projects ~55.3%. A scenario-based Monte Carlo simulation (75% business-as-usual, 11% acceleration, 7% slowdown, 7% partial disruption) yields a median of 55.32% with most of the distribution concentrated tightly around 55%. The narrow concentration reflects the remarkably stable injection pattern observed over the past two weeks. Downside risks (Hormuz LNG disruption, cold snaps) are modeled but reduced in weight since 2 months of Hormuz closure haven't visibly impacted Italian injection rates, and Italy's main gas supply is via unaffected pipeline from Algeria.

factors.description: Current AGSI data shows Italy at 51.38% as of May 4 with remarkably stable injection at 0.36 pp/day over last 7 days, projecting to ~55.3% by May 15.
Injection rates have accelerated from ~0.28 pp/day in early April to 0.37-0.38 pp/day now, stabilized for the last 5 days. Suggests current high rate may persist.

Strait of Hormuz closure disrupting ~20% of global LNG trade since early March. However, 2 months of closure haven't visibly impacted Italian injection rates, as main supply is via pipeline from Algeria.
EU 90% fill mandate by November 1 creates strong regulatory pressure to maintain high injection rates regardless of price.
May weather in Italy is warm with near-zero heating demand, virtually guaranteeing net injection every day of the forecast window.

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Italy's Natural Gas Storage Fill % on May 15, 2026**

**Central estimate: 55.3%** (median)

### Key Evidence
- **Starting point**: Italy at 51.38% full as of May 4, 2026 (confirmed from AGSI API)
- **Injection rate**: Very stable at 0.37-0.38 pp/day for the last 5-7 days (~765 GWh/day)
- **Time horizon**: 11 days remaining → linear projection gives 55.3%

### Distribution (from scenario-based Monte Carlo, 200K simulations)

| Percentile | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| P1 | 52.96% |
| P5 | 53.82% |
| P10 | 54.35% |
| P25 | 55.04% |
| P50 | 55.32% |
| P75 | 55.57% |
| P90 | 55.85% |
| P95 | 56.07% |
| P99 | 56.48% |

The distribution is relatively tight because:
1. We have 15 days of actual daily injection data showing remarkably stable rates
2. May is firmly in injection season with near-zero heating demand
3. The Hormuz closure (ongoing since March) hasn't visibly impacted Italian injection
4. Only 11 days of projection needed

The left tail (down to ~53%) accounts for potential supply disruptions or technical outages, while the right tail (~56.5%) accounts for possible injection acceleration.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) What percentage of Italy's natural gas storage capacity will be filled as of May 15, 2026, according to AGSI?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43400)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as the "Full (%)" value reported for Italy (country code IT) on the AGSI+ Data Overview page (https://agsi.gie.eu/data-overview/IT) for the gas day starting May 15, 2026 (i.e., the row labeled Gas Day Start 2026-05-15). The value will be entered as the number of percent (e.g., a reported "Full" value of 56.42% resolves as 56.42). If AGSI has not published a value for that gas day by May 17, 2026, the most recent prior gas day in May 2026 with a published value will be used. If AGSI is entirely unavailable, the equivalent figure from the GIE AGSI+ dashboard at https://agsi.gie.eu will be used as a fallback.
fine_print: Values are taken as published by AGSI without further adjustment. Revised "C" (confirmed) values supersede provisional "E" (estimated) or "N" (no data) values if a revision is published before resolution.

factors.description: Current AGSI data shows Italy at 51.38% as of May 4 with 11 days remaining. Recent 7-day injection rate is very stable at 0.36 pp/day, which projects to ~55.3% by May 15.
Injection rates have been accelerating: early April ~0.28 pp/day, now stabilized at ~0.37-0.38 pp/day. Last 3 days all at 765 GWh/day injection. Suggests the current high rate may persist.
Strait of Hormuz closure disrupting ~20% of global LNG trade. Italy partially reliant on LNG. Could constrain injection supply, pushing rate lower.
EU 90% fill mandate by November 1 creates strong regulatory pressure to maintain high injection rates regardless of price. Operators have incentive to keep injecting.
Italy energy rationing/lockdown reports suggest government awareness of supply stress but also demand reduction measures that could free up gas for storage.
May weather in Italy is warm - heating demand near zero, so virtually all gas goes to storage. Very unlikely to see net withdrawal days in mid-May.

input.anchor: Italy gas storage injection pattern from AGSI data: 14 days of consistent daily injection at 0.34 pp/day average, linear projection gives 55.2% (current 51.38% + 11 days × 0.34)
input.assessment:
This is a short-horizon measurement question with strong empirical anchoring. Italy's gas storage fill level as of May 15 depends on 11 more days of injection from the May 4 reading of 51.38%. The AGSI data shows remarkably stable injection rates, particularly in the last 5-7 days where rates have stabilized at 0.37-0.38 pp/day (~765 GWh/day). The main uncertainty is whether this rate continues or gets disrupted.

The scenario-based Monte Carlo incorporating four scenarios (business as usual 70%, cold snap 10%, acceleration 10%, partial disruption 10%) gives:
- Median: 55.29%
- Mean: 55.11%  
- 80% CI: 54.0% to 55.8%
- 95% CI: 53.4% to 56.0%

The distribution is moderately right-skewed toward the base case, with a fat left tail from disruption scenarios. The tail risks are plausible but unlikely: the Hormuz closure primarily affects oil and Qatari LNG, while Italy's main gas supply comes via pipeline from Algeria (TransMed) which is unaffected.

Key tension: the scenario-based model gives a wider distribution than pure random-walk extrapolation (which would give P5-P95 of ~55.0-55.5), but narrower than typical geopolitical uncertainty might suggest. Given that injection has been remarkably stable for 14 days and we're projecting only 11 days forward in the middle of injection season, the narrow-ish base case is well-justified.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is 55.2% from linear projection. Monte Carlo median is 55.29%. The distribution width (80% CI ~1.8 pp) reflects a blend of stable base-case extrapolation with 30% weight on disruption/acceleration scenarios. Random walk alone would give only ±0.23 pp - too narrow given regime change risks. But 1.8 pp over 11 days seems reasonable for a stable physical process with geopolitical tail risks.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Whether Hormuz closure will meaningfully constrain Italy's gas injection supply (main pipeline supply unaffected, but LNG shortfall could matter)
2. Whether the 765 GWh/day injection rate is sustainable or represents a temporary peak
3. Weather surprises (late cold snap requiring heating)
4. TTF price spikes making injection uneconomical
input.tool_audit: AGSI API: Excellent - provided 15 days of actual daily data directly. Web search: Useful for context (Hormuz, energy rationing news). Research sub-agents: Limited by tool restrictions but provided good structural background. Monte Carlo simulation: Core analytical tool, ran well.
input.process_reflection: The AGSI API access was the key enabler - getting 15 days of actual daily injection data made this a well-grounded empirical forecast rather than speculation. The research sub-agent couldn't access live data due to tool restrictions, but the structural background on historical patterns and geopolitical context was helpful. The Monte Carlo framework worked well for this type of short-horizon measurement question. The main friction was the research sub-agent being unable to access web sources, requiring direct fetch_url calls for live data.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**Summary:** This is a well-executed short-horizon numeric forecast with strong empirical anchoring from live AGSI API data.

**Evidence verification:**
- Factor 1: Italy at 51.38% on May 4, 7-day rate 0.36 pp/day → confirmed directly from AGSI API fetch (trace line 177-184) and code output showing "Recent 7-day mean: 0.3586 pp/day"
- Factor 2: Last 3 days at 765 GWh/day, acceleration from early April ~0.28 → confirmed from AGSI data table and daily change computations (0.29, 0.26, 0.28 early vs 0.37, 0.38, 0.38 recent)
- Factor 3: Hormuz closure disrupting LNG trade → confirmed from web search result (planet-today.com) and research sub-agent
- Factor 4: EU 90% fill mandate → confirmed from research entry citing EU Regulation 2022/1032
- Factor 5: Italy energy lockdown → confirmed from web search snippet from planet-today.com
- Factor 6: May warm weather, near-zero heating demand → reasonable domain knowledge

**No hallucinated evidence found.** All factor claims trace back to actual tool results.

**Double-counting (minor):** Factors 1 and 2 draw on the same AGSI injection rate data (Factor 1 uses 7-day average, Factor 2 highlights the acceleration trend). These are correlated signals from the same source, though the agent applies modest logits (+2.0 and +1.0) in the same direction. Since both point the same way and the combined effect still produces a reasonable center (55.3), this doesn't materially distort the forecast.

**Resolution criteria alignment:** The agent correctly targets the AGSI "Full (%)" value for Italy gas day 2026-05-15, uses the correct data source (agsi.gie.eu API), and understands the resolution mechanics. ✓

**Probability assessment:** The center of 55.3% with 80% CI ~54.0-55.8% is well-supported by the data. Linear projection from 51.38% + 11 days × 0.36 pp/day = 55.34%. The scenario-based Monte Carlo properly adds tail risk for supply disruption while keeping the base case dominant (70% weight). My independent estimate would also center around 55.2-55.4 given the extremely stable recent injection pattern. No meaningful divergence.

**One noted tension:** Factor 3 assigns -0.8 logit for Hormuz LNG disruption risk, but the live data shows Italy was injecting at 765 GWh/day on May 4 - two full months after the Hormuz closure began in early March - suggesting Italian gas supply is already coping (via pipeline from Algeria). This makes the downside scenario somewhat less likely than the 10% assigned. However, the factor's low confidence (0.4) partially accounts for this, and it does not significantly bias the final estimate.

**Counterargument quality:** Adequate - raises valid concerns about provisional "E" data revision and potential LNG supply disruption, though doesn't fully engage with the fact that 2 months of Hormuz closure haven't visibly impacted Italian injection.

**Confidence (0.65):** Appropriate for a well-anchored empirical question with genuine but limited geopolitical tail risks.

**Tool audit:** Agent claims no failures; metrics confirm 0 errors across 11 calls. ✓
